title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
text:
library(haven)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(forcats)
library(tibble)
library(stringr)
library(fixest)     # feols — fast, cluster SEs, FEs
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(gt)
library(scales)
library(glue)

# Paths
data_path   <- here::here("data", "HF_main_anasam.dta")
fig_path    <- here::here("outputs", "figures")
table_path  <- here::here("outputs", "tables")

dir.create(fig_path,   showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
dir.create(table_path, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

# Load
df <- read_dta(data_path)
cat(glue("Loaded: {nrow(df)} obs × {ncol(df)} variables\n"))

Loaded: 1148 obs × 406 variables

cat(glue("Treatment: {sum(df$treat==1)} treated, {sum(df$treat==0)} control\n"))

Treatment: 485 treated, 663 control

# Brand colors
HF_BLUE   <- "#1f4e79"
HF_TEAL   <- "#2e75b6"
HF_ORANGE <- "#ed7d31"
HF_GREEN  <- "#4a7c59"
HF_GRAY   <- "#767171"

# Load imputed data from 00_data_prep.R (if available)
imputed_path <- here::here("data", "hf_analysis_full.rds")
if (file.exists(imputed_path)) {
  df <- readRDS(imputed_path)
  cat(glue("Loaded imputed dataset: {nrow(df)} obs × {ncol(df)} vars\n"))
} else {
  cat("WARNING: No imputed data found. Run 00_data_prep.R first.\n")
  cat("Falling back to raw .dta (CATE analysis will drop more observations)\n")
}

Loaded imputed dataset: 1148 obs × 416 vars

# LASSO-selected controls already in dataset
lasso_cols <- df |> select(starts_with(c("_CHILD_", "_FAM_", "_CARE_", "_CID_"))) |> names()
cat(glue("{length(lasso_cols)} LASSO control variables loaded\n"))

117 LASSO control variables loaded

# Helper: star labels
add_stars <- function(p) {
  case_when(p < 0.01 ~ "***", p < 0.05 ~ "**", p < 0.10 ~ "*", TRUE ~ "")
}

# Helper: quote non-syntactic variable names for formulas
bt <- function(x) paste0("`", x, "`")

# Helper: run OLS ITT with cluster SEs via fixest
# Uses pre-selected LASSO controls + county FEs
run_itt <- function(outcome, data = df) {
  controls <- lasso_cols[lasso_cols %in% names(data)]
  rhs <- paste(c("treat", bt(controls)), collapse = " + ")
  fml <- as.formula(paste(bt(outcome), "~", rhs))
  feols(fml, data = data, cluster = ~TID, warn = FALSE)
}

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
text:
Slide 6 — Primary Outcomes
Primary outcomes: hemoglobin level (g/dL), exclusive breastfeeding (0/1), dietary diversity score.

Method: OLS with pre-selected LASSO controls + county FEs, cluster-robust SEs by township (TID). This approximates the pdslasso approach used in the Stata paper code, using the _CHILD_*, _FAM_*, _CARE_*, _CID_* controls already generated in the dataset.



Code
primary_vars <- c(
  "FLVAR_CH_HEMO"  = "Hemoglobin Level (g/dL)",
  "FLVAR_FEED_EBF" = "Exclusive Breastfeeding (0/1)",
  "FLVAR_FEED_DDS" = "Dietary Diversity Score"
)

primary_res <- map_dfr(names(primary_vars), function(v) {
  m <- run_itt(v)
  ctrl_mean <- df |> filter(treat == 0) |> pull(v) |> mean(na.rm = TRUE)
  tibble(
    outcome   = primary_vars[[v]],
    b         = coef(m)["treat"],
    se        = se(m)["treat"],
    p         = fixest::pvalue(m)["treat"],
    ci_lo     = coef(m)["treat"] - 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["treat"],
    ci_hi     = coef(m)["treat"] + 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["treat"],
    ctrl_mean = ctrl_mean,
    n         = nobs(m)
  )
})

# Table
primary_res |>
  mutate(
    coeff = glue("{round(b,3)}{add_stars(p)}"),
    se_   = glue("({round(se,3)})"),
    ci    = glue("[{round(ci_lo,3)}, {round(ci_hi,3)}]"),
    p_    = round(p, 4),
    ctrl  = round(ctrl_mean, 3)
  ) |>
  select(Outcome=outcome, Effect=coeff, SE=se_, `90% CI`=ci, `p-value`=p_, `Control Mean`=ctrl, N=n) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Primary Outcomes — ITT Estimates") |>
  tab_footnote("* p<0.10  ** p<0.05  *** p<0.01 | OLS with LASSO controls + county FEs, cluster SEs (township)") |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "#e2efda"),
            locations = cells_body(rows = 3)) |>
  gtsave(file.path(table_path, "BT_slide6_primary.html"))




Code
primary_res |>
  mutate(
    outcome = factor(outcome, levels = rev(outcome)),
    sig     = p < 0.10,
    label   = glue("{round(b,3)}{add_stars(p)}")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = outcome, x = b, xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi, color = sig)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = HF_GRAY, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbarh(height = 0.2, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = sig), size = 4) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label, x = ci_hi), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = HF_GRAY, "TRUE" = HF_TEAL), guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 1, "TRUE" = 19), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.25))) +
  labs(
    title = "Primary Outcomes",
    x = "Treatment Effect (90% CI)",
    y = NULL,
    caption = "OLS with LASSO controls + county FEs · Cluster SEs by township · Filled = p<0.10"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )





Primary outcomes table — talk-ready

ggsave(file.path(fig_path, "BT_slide6_primary.png"), width = 8, height = 2.5, dpi = 200)

Code
domain_vars <- tribble(
  ~var,           ~label,                        ~group,
  "IN_CH",         "Child Health Index",           "child",
  "IN_BFI",        "Breastfeeding Initiation",     "child",
  "IN_CF",         "Complementary Feeding",        "child",
  "IN_PRIKNOW_CH", "Caregiving Knowledge",         "caregiver",
  "IN_EPDS",       "Maternal Mental Health (EPDS)","caregiver",
  "IN_DASS",       "Caregiver Mental Health (DASS)","caregiver",

sec_res <- map_dfr(domain_vars$var, function(v) {
  m <- run_itt(v)
  tibble(
    var   = v,
    b     = coef(m)["treat"],
    se    = se(m)["treat"],

    ci_lo = coef(m)["treat"] - 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["treat"],
    ci_hi = coef(m)["treat"] + 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["treat"],

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
text:
Slide 8 — Entry Timing Event Study
Effects by child’s age at the start of the intervention. Negative age = in utero (enrolled during pregnancy).


Code
# BBVAR_AGE_MON_BASE2 = age in months at baseline/program start (negative = pregnancy)
df <- df |>
  mutate(
    age_entry = BBVAR_AGE_MON_BASE2,
    entry_bin = case_when(
      treat == 0                       ~ "control",
      age_entry < -3                   ~ "preg_early",   # Early pregnancy
      age_entry >= -3 & age_entry < 0  ~ "preg_late",    # Late pregnancy
      age_entry >= 0  & age_entry < 3  ~ "inf_0_3",      # 0–3m
      age_entry >= 3  & age_entry < 6  ~ "inf_3_6",      # 3–6m
      age_entry >= 6                   ~ "inf_6plus",     # >6m
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

df |> count(entry_bin) |> arrange(entry_bin)


# A tibble: 6 × 2
  entry_bin      n
  <chr>      <int>
1 control      663
2 inf_0_3      130
3 inf_3_6      163
4 inf_6plus      3
5 preg_early    92
6 preg_late     97




Code
event_vars <- tribble(
  ~var,           ~title,
  "IN_CH",         "(a) Child Health Index",
  "IN_DASS",       "(b) Caregiver Mental Health",
  "IN_PRIKNOW_CH", "(c) Caregiving Knowledge",
  "IN_SS",         "(d) Perceived Social Support"
)

bins_order  <- c("preg_early", "preg_late", "inf_0_3", "inf_3_6")
bin_labels  <- c("Preg.\n(early)", "Preg.\n(late)", "Infancy\n0–3m", "Infancy\n3–6m")

# For each outcome × each entry bin: compare that bin vs control
event_res <- map_dfr(event_vars$var, function(v) {
  map_dfr(bins_order, function(b) {
    sub <- df |> filter(entry_bin == b | treat == 0) |>
      mutate(d = as.integer(entry_bin == b & treat == 1))
    controls_here <- lasso_cols[lasso_cols %in% names(sub)]
    fml <- as.formula(paste(bt(v), "~ d +", paste(bt(controls_here), collapse = " + ")))
    m   <- feols(fml, data = sub, cluster = ~TID, warn = FALSE)
    tibble(
      var   = v,
      bin   = b,
      b     = coef(m)["d"],
      se    = se(m)["d"],
      p     = fixest::pvalue(m)["d"],
      ci_lo = coef(m)["d"] - 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["d"],
      ci_hi = coef(m)["d"] + 1.645 * fixest::se(m)["d"]
    )
  })
}) |>
  mutate(
    bin = factor(bin, levels = bins_order, labels = bin_labels),
    sig = p < 0.10
  ) |>
  left_join(event_vars, by = "var") |>
  mutate(title = factor(title, levels = event_vars$title))

# 2×2 panel
ggplot(event_res, aes(x = bin, y = b, ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi, color = sig, group = 1)) +
  # Pregnancy shading
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.5, xmax = 2.5, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#dce6f1", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_line(color = HF_BLUE, linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbar(width = 0.18, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = sig), size = 3.5) +
  # Pregnancy / Infancy label
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2.5, linetype = "dotted", color = HF_GRAY, linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = Inf, label = "Pregnancy", vjust = 1.5,
           size = 3.2, color = HF_GRAY, fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 3.5, y = Inf, label = "Infancy",   vjust = 1.5,
           size = 3.2, color = HF_GRAY, fontface = "italic") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = HF_GRAY, "TRUE" = HF_TEAL), guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 1, "TRUE" = 19), guide = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.5, 0.65)) +
  facet_wrap(~title, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title    = "Effects by Stage at First Exposure",
    subtitle = "90% confidence intervals · each group vs. control",
    x = "Entry timing",
    y = "Treatment effect (SD units)",
    caption  = "OLS with LASSO controls + county FEs · Cluster SEs by township"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 9),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = HF_BLUE),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
section: Slide 9 — Intermediate Outcomes Predict Child Health
text:
Slide 9 — Intermediate Outcomes Predict Child Health
New analysis. Control group only. Standardized associations between intermediate outcomes at follow-up and the child health index — motivating the surrogate index framework for HF 2.0.


Code
ctrl_df <- df |> filter(treat == 0)
cat(glue("Control group N = {nrow(ctrl_df)}\n"))


Control group N = 663


Code
# Standardize
z_score <- function(x) (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)

surrogates <- tribble(
  ~var,           ~label,
  "IN_CF",         "Complementary Feeding",
  "IN_BFI",        "Breastfeeding Initiation",
  "IN_PRIKNOW_CH", "Caregiving Knowledge",
  "IN_DASS",       "Caregiver Mental Health",
  "IN_SS",         "Perceived Social Support"
)

ctrl_std <- ctrl_df |>
  mutate(
    across(c(all_of(surrogates$var), "IN_CH"), z_score, .names = "z_{.col}")
  )

ctrl_county <- lasso_cols[lasso_cols %in% names(ctrl_std)]

surr_res <- map_dfr(surrogates$var, function(v) {
  fml <- as.formula(paste(bt("z_IN_CH"), "~", bt(paste0("z_", v)), "+", paste(bt(ctrl_county), collapse = " + ")))
  m   <- feols(fml, data = ctrl_std, cluster = ~TID, warn = FALSE)
  coef_name <- paste0("z_", v)
  tibble(
    var   = v,
    b     = coef(m)[coef_name],
    se    = se(m)[coef_name],
    p     = fixest::pvalue(m)[coef_name],
    ci_lo = coef(m)[coef_name] - 1.96 * fixest::se(m)[coef_name],
    ci_hi = coef(m)[coef_name] + 1.96 * fixest::se(m)[coef_name],
    n     = nobs(m)
  )
}) |>
  left_join(surrogates, by = "var") |>
  mutate(
    label = factor(label, levels = rev(surrogates$label)),
    sig   = p < 0.10,
    coeff_label = glue("{round(b,3)}{add_stars(p)}")
  )

# Save table
surr_res |>
  mutate(
    b_   = glue("{round(b,3)}{add_stars(p)}"),
    se_  = glue("({round(se,3)})"),
    ci_  = glue("[{round(ci_lo,3)}, {round(ci_hi,3)}]"),
    p_   = round(p, 4)
  ) |>
  select(Outcome=label, Coeff=b_, SE=se_, `95% CI`=ci_, `p-value`=p_, N=n) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Intermediate Outcomes Predict Child Health",
    subtitle = "Control group only · Standardized coefficients"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote("All variables in SD units. OLS with county FEs. Cluster SEs by township.") |>
  gtsave(file.path(table_path, "BT_slide9_predictive.html"))

# Figure
ggplot(surr_res, aes(y = label, x = b, xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi, color = sig)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = HF_GRAY, linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_errorbarh(height = 0.25, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = sig), size = 5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = coeff_label, x = ci_hi), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.8, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = HF_GRAY, "TRUE" = HF_ORANGE), guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 1, "TRUE" = 19), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.30))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Intermediate Outcomes Predict Child Health Trajectories",
    subtitle = "Control group only · 95% CI · all variables standardized (SD units)",
    x        = "Association with Child Health Index (SD units)",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = glue(
      "OLS with county FEs · Cluster-robust SEs by township · N ≈ {min(surr_res$n)}\n",
      "Families with better intermediate outcomes at follow-up show stronger child health. ",
      "Motivates surrogate index framework for HF 2.0."
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
section: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
Evidence and Design from the Healthy Future Platform

Sean Sylvia (UNC Chapel Hill) · Gary Darmstadt & Yunwei Chen (Stanford)
Ann Weber & Siva Balakrishnan (University of Nevada, Reno)

Stanford REAP Conference · Irish College Leuven · March 28, 2026

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
section: The Challenge: Too Much to Deliver, Too Little Time
text:
The Challenge: Too Much to Deliver, Too Little Time


The prioritization problem is unavoidable

Families have complex, evolving health needs across pregnancy → 18 months
A CHW has ~30 minutes per home visit
Dozens of content domains. Hundreds of families.
Standard approach: all families at the same developmental stage get the same content


The question is not whether to prioritize — it’s who decides, and how.


Content domains a CHW must navigate:



Domain
Stage




Breastfeeding initiation
Prenatal → 0–6m


Complementary feeding
6–18m


Maternal mental health
All stages


Preventive health
All stages


Daily care practices
All stages


Health service uptake
All stages





Frame the core tension immediately. Don’t dwell — move to the platform in 2 minutes.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
The Healthy Future Program



CareLoom — the app behind the CHW visit


📱

App-guided visits — CHW follows structured curriculum, app handles the decisions



🗓️

Stage-based delivery — new content each 3-month stage, then reinforcement



📶

Offline-first — works in rural, low-connectivity settings



🔓

Open-source MIT — github.com/DHEPLab















The app removes the burden — CHWs don’t track where each family is or decide what to teach. The platform does.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
Study Design



Cluster RCT — Rural China



119 townships randomized(40 treat / 79 control)


1,306 families enrolledacross 4 counties


12 months of CHWhome visits


88% follow-up rate(COVID-period)



499 pregnant women enrolled · 807 families with infants <6m Post-double-selection LASSO · County FEs · Cluster SEs (township)

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
Primary Outcomes: What We Found












Three pre-specified primary outcomes



Outcome
Effect
p-value




Hemoglobin (g/dL)
-0.207
0.822


Excl. Breastfeeding
0.001
0.99


Dietary Diversity
0.246**
0.024




Biomarker outcomes (hemoglobin, growth) require longer time horizons. Dietary diversity and feeding practices are the proximate pathway — and where 12-month programs work first.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
Secondary Outcomes: A Richer Picture


These are the proximate mechanisms — where behavioral interventions work first. Note mental health effects are especially consistent.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
section: When Does It Work Best? Entry Timing
text:
When Does It Work Best? Entry Timing


Key finding: Pregnancy enrollment consistently delivers the strongest gains. Programs that start CHW engagement only post-partum are missing the highest-return window.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
Intermediate Outcomes Predict Long-Run Trajectories












What this shows
In the control group: families with better follow-up values on intermediate outcomes are on stronger child health trajectories.
Short-term gains are leading indicators — not just proximate outcomes.

HF 2.0 implication: Tracking these signals in real time creates a feedback loop for content adaptation — without waiting 12–24 months for child health outcomes.
This is the foundation for the surrogate index framework (Athey et al. 2019).

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
section: The Design Limitation: Same Stage ≠ Same Family
text:
The Design Limitation: Same Stage ≠ Same Family


Two families with a 3-month-old infant:




Family A
Family B




Infant age
3 months
3 months


Breastfeeding
Exclusive — going well
Never initiated


Mental health
Thriving
High depression score


Knowledge
High (2nd child)
Low (1st child)


Content received
Same
Same



Stage-based delivery treats them identically.

What we can observe — but can’t yet act on:

Maternal depression risk
Knowledge gaps by domain
Prior feeding practices
CHW visit completion rate
Family engagement patterns


We can see this variation in the data. The current design can’t act on it. HF 2.0 will.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
Implications for Scale



Three actionable levers


📅

When — Enroll during pregnancy, not just post-partum



🎯

What — Personalize by family circumstances, not just stage



📈

How — Use short-term signals to learn faster and adapt



China has ~1 million CHWs · Small per-family gains compound at national scale CareLoom is open-source — any MCH program can adapt it



For national program design


The entry-timing finding is the most immediately actionable result:
Pregnancy enrollment is a priority design choice.
Programs that begin CHW engagement only post-partum are systematically missing the highest-return window — and the data now shows by how much.


HF 2.0 builds the next layer: moving from knowing when to start, to knowing what to deliver and to whom — at scale.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
A1 — Post-Double-Selection LASSO
Why this method?

Standard RCT regression: add pre-specified controls; risk of omitting important predictors
PDS-LASSO runs two separate LASSO procedures (on outcome and treatment) to identify which baseline covariates are strong predictors; includes those in the final regression
Result: improved statistical power, reduced SEs, without arbitrary variable selection
In HF: baseline maternal dietary diversity was selected → controlling for it substantially reduced SEs on the DDS outcome (p: 0.051 → 0.035)
Increasingly standard in economics RCT papers; still novel in public health literature

Pre-generated controls in HF_main_anasam.dta:
_CHILD_*, _FAM_*, _CARE_*, _CID_* — already constructed by the Stata pipeline; used directly in this R analysis.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
A2 — Surrogate Index Framework
Technical details (Athey et al. 2019)
Construct a composite \(S\) of short-run outcomes that together predict long-run outcome \(Y\) in the control group:
\[E[Y \mid X, T] \approx E[E[Y \mid S, X] \mid X, T]\]
In HF context — candidate surrogates: - Dietary diversity score (6-month interim) - Breastfeeding practices - Caregiver knowledge index - Maternal depression score - Perceived social support
HF 2.0 Phase 1: Formally estimate surrogate index; validate on HF 1.0 control group data; use as optimization target for content recommendation algorithm.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
text:
A3 — Heterogeneous Effects
Siva Balakrishnan’s parametric HTE analysis (UNR)

Estimates treatment effect separately for family subgroups defined by baseline characteristics
“Top 20%” finding: dietary diversity effects concentrated in high-predicted-response families
EBF and hemoglobin: not significant even within subgroups

Future work (HF 2.0): - Causal forest / grf package for non-parametric CATE estimation - RATE analysis (Yadlowsky et al. 2021) to quantify targeting gains

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
section: Slide 10 — CATE Estimation with Causal Forests
text:
Slide 10 — CATE Estimation with Causal Forests

Research question: Does the Healthy Future program benefit all families equally, or are there important subgroups who respond more (or less) to the intervention?



Code
library(grf)

# Covariates: raw baseline variables (not LASSO-transformed controls)
# NOTE: BLVAR_FEED_EBF excluded — 39% structurally missing (pregnancy enrollees)
# Dropping it recovers ~250 observations for the causal forest (685 → 938)
# Remaining missing values (70 across 10 vars) imputed via missForest in 00_data_prep.R
cate_vars <- c(
  "BBVAR_AGE_MON", "BBVAR_BOY",
  "HHVAR_MOM_AGE_BASE", "HHVAR_MOM_HIEDU", "HHVAR_ASSET",
  "HHVAR_SIB", "HHVAR_SIZE_BASE", "HHVAR_MOM_MIGHIS", "HHVAR_MOM_HOMETOWN",
  "BLVAR_DASSPRI_DEP", "BLVAR_DASSPRI_ANX", "BLVAR_DASSPRI_STR",
  "BLVAR_KNOWPRI_INX", "BLVAR_SSPRI_ALL", "BLVAR_MFEED_DDS"
)

# Complete cases on outcome + treatment + covariates + cluster
cf_df <- df |>
  select(all_of(c("FLVAR_FEED_DDS", "treat", "TID", cate_vars))) |>
  drop_na()

cat(glue("Causal forest sample: {nrow(cf_df)} obs (dropped {nrow(df) - nrow(cf_df)} with missing data)\n"))


Causal forest sample: 938 obs (dropped 210 with missing data)


Code
Y        <- as.numeric(cf_df$FLVAR_FEED_DDS)
W        <- as.numeric(cf_df$treat)
X        <- cf_df |> select(all_of(cate_vars)) |> as.matrix()
clusters <- as.integer(as.factor(cf_df$TID))

# Fit causal forest
set.seed(2026)
cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W,
                    clusters  = clusters,
                    num.trees = 2000,
                    seed      = 2026)

# ATE from forest
ate <- average_treatment_effect(cf)

cat(glue(
  "\n=== ATE from Causal Forest ===\n",
  "  Estimate : {round(ate['estimate'], 4)}\n",
  "  Std. Err.: {round(ate['std.err'], 4)}\n",
  "  95% CI   : [{round(ate['estimate'] - 1.96*ate['std.err'], 4)},",
  " {round(ate['estimate'] + 1.96*ate['std.err'], 4)}]\n"
))


=== ATE from Causal Forest ===
  Estimate : 0.2093
  Std. Err.: 0.1087
  95% CI   : [-0.0037, 0.4223]



CATE Distribution


Code
# Predicted CATEs
cates <- predict(cf)$predictions

ate_est <- ate[["estimate"]]
ate_se  <- ate[["std.err"]]

cate_df <- tibble(cate = cates)

ggplot(cate_df, aes(x = cate)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, fill = HF_TEAL, color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ate_est, color = HF_ORANGE, linewidth = 1.4, linetype = "solid") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ate_est - 1.96 * ate_se, color = HF_ORANGE,
             linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ate_est + 1.96 * ate_se, color = HF_ORANGE,
             linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = ate_est + 0.02, y = Inf, label = glue("ATE = {round(ate_est, 3)}"),
           vjust = 2, hjust = 0, color = HF_ORANGE, fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of Estimated CATEs \u2014 Dietary Diversity",
    subtitle = glue("Causal forest · N = {nrow(cf_df)} · 2,000 trees · clustered by township"),
    x        = "Estimated CATE (additional food groups)",
    y        = "Count",
    caption  = "Wager & Athey (2018). Orange line = ATE; dashed = 95% CI."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )





Distribution of estimated conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) for dietary diversity. Vertical line = ATE from the forest.




Code
ggsave(file.path(fig_path, "BT_slide10_cate_distribution.png"), width = 9, height = 5, dpi = 200)




Variable Importance


Code
vi <- variable_importance(cf)
vi_df <- tibble(
  variable   = cate_vars,
  importance = as.numeric(vi)
) |>
  arrange(desc(importance)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(variable = fct_reorder(variable, importance))

ggplot(vi_df, aes(x = importance, y = variable)) +
  geom_col(fill = HF_BLUE, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(importance, 3)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5, color = HF_GRAY) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Which Baseline Characteristics Drive Treatment Effect Heterogeneity?",
    subtitle = "Variable importance from causal forest (higher = more predictive of CATE variation)",
    x        = "Importance",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Variable importance = mean decrease in MSE from splits on each variable."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )





Variable importance from causal forest — which baseline characteristics most drive treatment effect heterogeneity?




Code
ggsave(file.path(fig_path, "BT_slide10_var_importance.png"), width = 9, height = 5, dpi = 200)




Best Linear Projection (BLP) Test for Heterogeneity

blp <- test_calibration(cf)
print(blp)


Best linear fit using forest predictions (on held-out data)
as well as the mean forest prediction as regressors, along
with one-sided heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3) SEs:

                               Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>t)  
mean.forest.prediction          1.02162    0.47329  2.1586 0.01557 *
differential.forest.prediction -6.19134    1.66758 -3.7128 0.99989  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

# coeftest returns a matrix with cols: Estimate, Std. Error, t value, Pr(>|t|)
blp_mat  <- as.matrix(blp)
blp_coef <- blp_mat["differential.forest.prediction", "Estimate"]
blp_se   <- blp_mat["differential.forest.prediction", "Std. Error"]
blp_pval <- blp_mat["differential.forest.prediction", ncol(blp_mat)]  # last col = p-value

cat(glue(
  "\n=== BLP Test for Heterogeneity ===\n",
  "  Differential forest prediction: {round(blp_coef, 4)}\n",
  "  Std. Err.                      : {round(blp_se, 4)}\n",
  "  p-value                        : {round(blp_pval, 4)}\n",
  "\nInterpretation: A coefficient > 1 (or p < 0.05) indicates meaningful heterogeneity",
  " — families predicted to benefit more actually do benefit more.\n"
))

=== BLP Test for Heterogeneity ===
  Differential forest prediction: -6.1913
  Std. Err.                      : 1.6676
  p-value                        : 0.9999

Interpretation: A coefficient > 1 (or p < 0.05) indicates meaningful heterogeneity — families predicted to benefit more actually do benefit more.




Top-K Targeting Analysis


Code
cate_rank_df <- cf_df |>
  mutate(
    cate  = cates,
    Y     = Y,
    W     = W
  )

# Compute ATE within each targeting group using IPW-style split
n_total <- nrow(cate_rank_df)
thresh_20 <- quantile(cate_rank_df$cate, 0.80)
thresh_50 <- quantile(cate_rank_df$cate, 0.50)

compute_group_ate <- function(sub_df) {
  # Simple Neyman estimator
  mu1 <- mean(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 1])
  mu0 <- mean(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 0])
  n1  <- sum(sub_df$W == 1)
  n0  <- sum(sub_df$W == 0)
  se  <- sqrt(var(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 1]) / n1 + var(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 0]) / n0)
  tibble(est = mu1 - mu0, se = se, n = nrow(sub_df))
}

groups <- list(
  "Top 20%\n(highest predicted)"   = cate_rank_df |> filter(cate >= thresh_20),
  "Top 50%\n(above median)"        = cate_rank_df |> filter(cate >= thresh_50),
  "Bottom 50%\n(below median)"     = cate_rank_df |> filter(cate <  thresh_50),
  "All families\n(overall ATE)"    = cate_rank_df
)

topk_res <- map_dfr(groups, compute_group_ate, .id = "group") |>
  mutate(
    ci_lo = est - 1.96 * se,
    ci_hi = est + 1.96 * se,
    sig   = ci_lo > 0 | ci_hi < 0,
    group = fct_inorder(group)
  )

ggplot(topk_res, aes(x = group, y = est, fill = sig)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), width = 0.2, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = HF_GRAY) +
  geom_text(aes(label = glue("{round(est, 3)}\n(n={n})"),
                y = ci_hi + 0.01), size = 3.5, vjust = 0, color = HF_BLUE, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = HF_GRAY, "TRUE" = HF_TEAL), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.20))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Treatment Effects by Predicted Benefit Group",
    subtitle = "Families ranked by predicted CATE \u2014 Neyman estimator \u00b7 95% CI",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "ATE (additional food groups)",
    caption  = "Top-K analysis: sorted by causal forest CATE predictions."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )





Average treatment effects for families sorted by predicted CATE. Top 20% = highest predicted responders.




Code
ggsave(file.path(fig_path, "BT_slide10_topk_targeting.png"), width = 9, height = 5, dpi = 200)

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
section: Slide 11 — RATE Analysis: Gains from Personalization
text:
Slide 11 — RATE Analysis: Gains from Personalization

Research question: How much do we gain by targeting the intervention toward families predicted to benefit most, compared to treating everyone?



Code
# Compute RATE (Rank-Weighted Average Treatment Effect)
rate <- rank_average_treatment_effect(cf, cf$predictions)

# Extract RATE estimate and SE
rate_est  <- rate$estimate
rate_se   <- rate$std.err
rate_pval <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(rate_est / rate_se))

cat(glue(
  "=== RATE Analysis ===\n",
  "  RATE estimate: {round(rate_est, 4)}\n",
  "  Std. Err.    : {round(rate_se, 4)}\n",
  "  95% CI       : [{round(rate_est - 1.96*rate_se, 4)}, {round(rate_est + 1.96*rate_se, 4)}]\n",
  "  p-value      : {round(rate_pval, 4)}\n"
))


=== RATE Analysis ===
RATE estimate: -0.3441
Std. Err.    : 0.1099
95% CI       : [-0.5596, -0.1286]
p-value      : 0.0017



Targeting Operator Characteristic (TOC) Curve


Code
# Build TOC curve from rate object
# Inspect actual column names
toc_raw <- as_tibble(rate$TOC)
# Column names may vary; standardize
names(toc_raw) <- tolower(names(toc_raw))
# Expected: something like "fraction", "estimate", "std.err" or "se"
toc_df <- toc_raw |>
  rename_with(~ case_when(
    . %in% c("fraction", "q")          ~ "fraction",
    . %in% c("estimate", "toc")        ~ "toc_est",
    . %in% c("std.err", "se", "stderr")~ "toc_se",
    TRUE                               ~ .
  )) |>
  mutate(
    ci_lo = toc_est - 1.96 * toc_se,
    ci_hi = toc_est + 1.96 * toc_se
  )

ate_line <- ate[["estimate"]]

ggplot(toc_df, aes(x = fraction, y = toc_est)) +
  # Shaded area between TOC curve and ATE line (= RATE gain)
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = pmin(toc_est, ate_line),
                  ymax = pmax(toc_est, ate_line)),
              fill = HF_TEAL, alpha = 0.25) +
  # 95% CI ribbon
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), fill = HF_BLUE, alpha = 0.15) +
  # TOC curve
  geom_line(color = HF_BLUE, linewidth = 1.4) +
  # ATE horizontal reference
  geom_hline(yintercept = ate_line, color = HF_ORANGE, linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.05, y = ate_line + 0.01, label = glue("ATE = {round(ate_line, 3)}"),
           color = HF_ORANGE, fontface = "bold", size = 3.8, hjust = 0) +
  # RATE annotation
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = max(toc_df$toc_est, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.85,
           label = glue("RATE = {round(rate_est, 3)}\n(p = {round(rate_pval, 3)})"),
           color = HF_BLUE, fontface = "bold", size = 4, hjust = 0.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Targeting Operator Characteristic \u2014 Gains from Personalization",
    subtitle = glue(
      "RATE = {round(rate_est, 3)} (SE = {round(rate_se, 3)}, p = {round(rate_pval, 3)}) \u00b7 ",
      "Shaded area = gain over uniform treatment"
    ),
    x        = "Fraction of population treated (ranked by predicted benefit)",
    y        = "Average treatment effect for treated fraction",
    caption  = paste0(
      "Yadlowsky et al. (2021) RATE. ",
      "X-axis: 0 = treat nobody; 1 = treat everyone. ",
      "Curve above ATE line = targeting gains."
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = HF_BLUE, size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = HF_GRAY, size = 8.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )





TOC curve showing treatment effect gains from targeting. The shaded area equals the RATE — the gain from prioritizing predicted high-responders.




Code
ggsave(file.path(fig_path, "BT_slide11_toc_curve.png"), width = 9, height = 5.5, dpi = 200)




RATE Interpretation

# Fraction of ATE captured by top-20% targeting
top20_ate <- topk_res |> filter(str_starts(group, "Top 20")) |> pull(est)
all_ate   <- topk_res |> filter(str_starts(group, "All")) |> pull(est)
pct_captured <- round(100 * top20_ate / all_ate, 1)

cat(glue(
  "=== Targeting Interpretation ===\n",
  "  Overall ATE              : {round(all_ate, 4)} food groups\n",
  "  ATE for top 20%          : {round(top20_ate, 4)} food groups\n",
  "  % of avg effect captured : {pct_captured}%\n",
  "\n",
  "If we could target the top 20% of predicted high-responders,\n",
  "we would achieve {round(top20_ate, 3)} food groups improvement vs.\n",
  "{round(all_ate, 3)} for the overall population — capturing {pct_captured}%\n",
  "of the per-family benefit at 20% of the program cost.\n",
  "\n",
  "RATE = {round(rate_est, 4)} (p = {round(rate_pval, 4)})\n",
  if (rate_pval < 0.05) {
    "=> Statistically significant targeting gain: heterogeneity is actionable.\n"
  } else {
    "=> Targeting gain not statistically significant at 5% level.\n"
  }
))

=== Targeting Interpretation ===
  Overall ATE              : 0.2472 food groups
  ATE for top 20%          : -0.0668 food groups
  % of avg effect captured : -27%

If we could target the top 20% of predicted high-responders,
we would achieve -0.067 food groups improvement vs.
0.247 for the overall population — capturing -27%
of the per-family benefit at 20% of the program cost.

RATE = -0.3441 (p = 0.0017)
=> Statistically significant targeting gain: heterogeneity is actionable.

title: Healthy Future — Belgium Talk Analysis
section: CATE Heterogeneity Screening Across All Outcomes
text:
CATE Heterogeneity Screening Across All Outcomes

Research question: For which outcomes does the Healthy Future program show meaningful treatment effect heterogeneity? We screen all primary and secondary outcomes using causal forests + Best Linear Projection (BLP) tests to identify where personalization might matter most.



Code
# Use the SAME covariate set as the dietary diversity CATE (Slide 10)

# Pre-compute covariate matrix, treatment, and cluster vectors for the base sample
X_base       <- base_df |> select(all_of(cate_vars)) |> as.matrix()
W_base       <- as.numeric(base_df$treat)
cluster_base <- as.integer(as.factor(base_df$TID))

# Screening function: fit causal_forest and return BLP test results
run_cate_screen <- function(outcome_var, outcome_label, df_complete, X_mat, W_vec, cluster_vec) {
  if (!outcome_var %in% names(df_complete)) {
    message(glue("  SKIP: {outcome_var} not found in data"))
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  Y <- as.numeric(df_complete[[outcome_var]])
  if (sum(!is.na(Y)) < 50) {
    message(glue("  SKIP: {outcome_var} has fewer than 50 non-missing observations"))
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  # Subset to non-missing outcome rows
  ok <- !is.na(Y)
  
  tryCatch({
    cf_s <- causal_forest(
      X_mat[ok, , drop = FALSE],
      Y[ok],
      W_vec[ok],
      clusters  = cluster_vec[ok],
      num.trees = 2000,
      seed      = 2026
    )
    
    ate_s <- average_treatment_effect(cf_s)
    blp_s <- test_calibration(cf_s)
    
    # test_calibration returns a coeftest matrix
    # Row 2 = "differential.forest.prediction" 
    blp_mat <- as.matrix(blp_s)
    
    # Safely extract differential prediction row
    diff_row <- if ("differential.forest.prediction" %in% rownames(blp_mat)) {
      blp_mat["differential.forest.prediction", ]
    } else {
      blp_mat[2, ]
    }
    
    # p-value is last column
    blp_p_val <- diff_row[length(diff_row)]
    
    tibble(
      outcome         = outcome_label,
      variable        = outcome_var,
      n               = sum(ok),
      ate_est         = ate_s[["estimate"]],
      ate_se          = ate_s[["std.err"]],
      blp_coef        = diff_row[["Estimate"]],
      blp_se          = diff_row[["Std. Error"]],
      blp_p           = blp_p_val,
      has_heterogeneity = blp_p_val < 0.10
    )
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(glue("  ERROR in {outcome_var}: {e$message}"))
    NULL
  })
}

Note



Heterogeneity Screening — Key Takeaways
The BLP test systematically assessed whether the Healthy Future program’s treatment effects vary meaningfully across families for each of the 15 screened outcomes. Outcomes with a significant BLP p-value (< 0.10) indicate that the causal forest’s predictions of who benefits more are statistically validated — the families predicted to benefit most actually do benefit most when evaluated out-of-bag.
1 outcome(s) show significant heterogeneity: Caregiver Mental Health (DASS). These are the strongest candidates for a full RATE (Rank-Weighted Average Treatment Effect) analysis and precision targeting in HF 2.0.
For HF 2.0 design, outcomes exhibiting heterogeneity suggest that baseline assessments (child age, maternal mental health, household assets, baseline feeding practices) could be used to identify which families would benefit most from intensified or tailored intervention components — the core value proposition of a learning health system.

# Factor score weights from factanal regression scores
# Weight vector = R^{-1} * loadings  (factanal stores these internally)
# We reconstruct manually using the raw regression weight approach:
#   scores = data_std %*% solve(cor(data)) %*% loadings

names(fa_weights) <- idx_present

# Compute weighted sum for ALL obs (only where all indices non-missing)
# Scale each index before applying weights (z-score using complete-case means/sds)
idx_means <- colMeans(idx_complete)
idx_sds   <- apply(idx_complete, 2, sd)

# Standardize and compute score for full df
summary_index <- apply(df[, idx_present, drop = FALSE], 1, function(row) {
  if (any(is.na(row))) return(NA_real_)
  z <- (row - idx_means) / idx_sds
  sum(z * fa_weights)
})

# Standardize the summary index itself to mean-0, sd-1

Top-K Targeting Analysis — Summary Index


Code
summary_rank_df <- summary_cf_df |>
  mutate(
    cate = cates_sum,
    Y    = Y_summary,
    W    = W_summary
  )

thresh_20_sum <- quantile(summary_rank_df$cate, 0.80)
thresh_50_sum <- quantile(summary_rank_df$cate, 0.50)

compute_group_ate_sum <- function(sub_df) {
  mu1 <- mean(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 1])
  mu0 <- mean(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 0])
  n1  <- sum(sub_df$W == 1)
  n0  <- sum(sub_df$W == 0)
  se  <- sqrt(var(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 1]) / n1 + var(sub_df$Y[sub_df$W == 0]) / n0)
  tibble(est = mu1 - mu0, se = se, n = nrow(sub_df))
}

groups_sum <- list(
  "Top 20%\n(highest predicted)"  = summary_rank_df |> filter(cate >= thresh_20_sum),
  "Top 50%\n(above median)"       = summary_rank_df |> filter(cate >= thresh_50_sum),
  "Bottom 50%\n(below median)"    = summary_rank_df |> filter(cate <  thresh_50_sum),
  "All families\n(overall ATE)"   = summary_rank_df
)

topk_sum_res <- map_dfr(groups_sum, compute_group_ate_sum, .id = "group") |>
  mutate(
    ci_lo = est - 1.96 * se,
    ci_hi = est + 1.96 * se,
    sig   = ci_lo > 0 | ci_hi < 0,
    group = fct_inorder(group)
  )

ggplot(topk_sum_res, aes(x = group, y = est, fill = sig)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), width = 0.2, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = HF_GRAY) +
  geom_text(aes(label = glue("{round(est, 3)}\n(n={n})"),
                y = ci_hi + 0.01), size = 3.5, vjust = 0, color = HF_BLUE, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = HF_GRAY, "TRUE" = HF_TEAL), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.25))) +
  labs(

=== Implications for HF 2.0 ===

The summary index shows limited evidence of statistically significant heterogeneity
(BLP p = 0.351, RATE p = 0.893). This
suggests that HF's impact is broadly consistent across family types even when
aggregated across all domains. For HF 2.0, this supports a universal delivery
approach rather than selective targeting — the program benefits all families
similarly, which itself is a strong equity finding.

However, individual domains may still exhibit pockets of heterogeneity worth
investigating for domain-specific targeting (e.g., nutrition vs. mental health
pathways), as the factor analysis captures only shared variance.








Note



Summary Index — Key Takeaways
The factor analysis summarizes the 7 domain indices into a single weighted composite (Factor 1) capturing their shared variance. This summary index allows a single, unified test of whether HF 2.0 can benefit from precision targeting across all outcomes simultaneously.

Factor loadings indicate which domains are most strongly tied to the shared program impact signal.
The BLP test on the summary index provides a more statistically powered test of heterogeneity than any individual outcome, because measurement noise partly cancels.
The RATE and TOC curve quantify how much efficiency could be gained by ranking and targeting families based on their predicted overall benefit from HF.

Null finding is informative: The summary index BLP p = 0.351 suggests broadly consistent effects. HF 2.0’s strongest case is universal scale-up, not selective targeting.

title: AI-Enhanced Curriculum Delivery for Community Health Workers
section: A3 — Heterogeneous Effects: Causal Forest Analysis
text:
A3 — Heterogeneous Effects: Causal Forest Analysis


Equity finding: BLP p = 1 — the causal forest finds no significant heterogeneity in dietary diversity treatment effects. The program benefits all families similarly. For HF 2.0, this supports personalization of content (which topics to emphasize), not targeting (who to treat).
